learning_objectives: Define artificial intelligence and distinguish between narrow AI and general AI
Trace the history of AI in education from PLATO to large language models
Identify current AI applications across the educational lifecycle
readings: Holmes, W. et al. - Artificial Intelligence in Education Ch. 1-2
Luckin, R. - Machine Learning and Human Intelligence Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: AI in education is not new. Intelligent tutoring systems have existed since the 1970s. What is new is the scale, capability, and accessibility of modern AI. This session grounds the hype in history and evidence.
key_concepts: Narrow AI vs. general AI: what current systems can and cannot do
History: PLATO, SCHOLAR, cognitive tutors, MOOCs, LLMs
Current AI applications: adaptive learning, automated grading, chatbots, content generation
AI across the lifecycle: admissions, instruction, assessment, student support, administration
The AI hype cycle in education: separating promise from reality
lecture_notes.demonstration: AI tool exploration: test three AI-powered educational tools (intelligent tutor, writing assistant, automated feedback) and document their capabilities and limitations
setup_requirements: Access to AI educational tools for testing

review.value: ITS provide individualized instruction by modeling student knowledge, selecting appropriate problems, and providing targeted feedback. Decades of research show effect sizes of 0.4-1.0 SD improvement over conventional instruction.
Large language models (GPT, Claude) can generate content, provide feedback, and answer questions, but they hallucinate, lack pedagogical models, and cannot verify the correctness of their own outputs

learning_objectives: Explain how adaptive learning systems model student knowledge
Compare Bayesian knowledge tracing, item response theory, and deep knowledge tracing
Evaluate the effectiveness of commercial adaptive learning platforms
readings: Corbett, A. & Anderson, J. - Knowledge Tracing (original paper)

lecture_notes.introduction: Adaptive learning systems adjust the content, difficulty, sequence, or pacing of instruction based on a model of what the student knows. They represent the most mature application of AI in education, with decades of research behind them.
key_concepts: Student modeling: representing what a learner knows and does not know
Bayesian knowledge tracing: probabilistic model of skill mastery
Item response theory: estimating ability from response patterns
Deep knowledge tracing: neural network approaches to student modeling
Commercial platforms: ALEKS, Knewton, DreamBox, Carnegie Learning
lecture_notes.demonstration: Work through 15 minutes of ALEKS (math) and analyze how the system adapts: what data does it collect? How does it choose the next question? When does it decide you've mastered a concept?

review.value: Models each skill as a hidden variable (mastered or not). Updates probability of mastery based on observed correct/incorrect responses. Parameters: prior knowledge, learn rate, guess probability, slip probability.

Randomized controlled trials show ALEKS produces significant gains in math achievement, particularly for students starting with weaker preparation. Effect depends on implementation quality and student engagement.

learning_objectives: Explain how NLP powers automated essay scoring and feedback
Evaluate the accuracy and limitations of AI writing feedback tools
Design appropriate uses of LLMs in educational contexts
readings: Shermis, M. & Burstein, J. - Automated Essay Evaluation Ch. 1-3
Kasneci, E. et al. - ChatGPT for Good? On Opportunities and Challenges of LLMs for Education

lecture_notes.introduction: Natural language processing enables AI to read, evaluate, and generate text. In education, this powers automated essay scoring, intelligent writing tutors, chatbot assistants, and content generation. The capabilities are impressive; the limitations are critical to understand.
key_concepts: Automated essay scoring: feature-based and neural approaches
Formative writing feedback: tools like Turnitin Feedback Studio, Grammarly, ETS Criterion
LLM capabilities: question answering, explanation, content generation, tutoring
LLM limitations: hallucination, lack of pedagogical models, bias, inability to verify
Prompt engineering for educational applications
lecture_notes.demonstration: LLM teaching experiment: prompt an LLM to tutor you on a topic you know well. Identify instances of accurate teaching, hallucination, and pedagogically poor responses
setup_requirements: Access to an LLM (Claude or ChatGPT)
Evaluation rubric for AI tutoring quality

review.value: Automated essay scoring systems achieve inter-rater reliability comparable to human-human agreement for holistic scoring, but struggle with content accuracy, originality, and argumentation quality
LLMs generate plausible-sounding but factually incorrect information with high confidence; this is particularly dangerous in educational contexts where students cannot verify accuracy

learning_objectives: Identify ethical risks of AI in educational settings
Evaluate bias in AI systems used for educational decisions
Apply ethical frameworks to AI implementation decisions
readings: UNESCO - Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (education sections)

lecture_notes.introduction: AI in education raises profound ethical questions: Who decides what the AI optimizes for? Whose data trains it? What happens when it is wrong? Who is accountable? This session examines these questions through concrete cases.
key_concepts: Algorithmic bias: training data reflects and amplifies existing inequities
Privacy and surveillance: continuous data collection on student behavior
Transparency and explainability: black-box models making high-stakes decisions
Autonomy and agency: does AI empower or diminish learner autonomy?
Accountability: when an AI system causes harm, who is responsible?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Ethical case analysis: examine three real-world cases of AI in education (proctoring bias, predictive analytics labeling, automated grading errors) using an ethical analysis framework
setup_requirements: Ethical analysis framework (consequentialist, deontological, virtue ethics)
Discussion ground rules for sensitive topics

review.value: AI proctoring systems have shown higher false-positive rates for students of color; adaptive learning systems trained on majority populations may not serve marginalized learners equitably
The shift from assessing learning outcomes to monitoring learning behaviors (keystroke dynamics, eye tracking, facial expressions) raises fundamental questions about student privacy and autonomy

learning_objectives: Design learning experiences that appropriately integrate AI tools
Apply human-centered AI design principles to educational contexts
Create AI-use policies for educational settings
readings: ISTE - AI in Education Policy Framework

lecture_notes.introduction: The question is not whether to use AI in education but how to use it well. Human-centered AI design keeps the educator and learner in control, using AI to augment human capabilities rather than replace human judgment.
key_concepts: Human-in-the-loop: AI assists, humans decide
AI as tutor, tool, and tutee (Jonassen's framework adapted)
Designing for AI transparency: students know when they are interacting with AI
AI literacy: what students need to know about how AI works
Policy design: acceptable use, disclosure requirements, academic integrity
lecture_notes.demonstration: AI policy design workshop: draft an AI-use policy for a university department that addresses academic integrity, disclosure, equity, and pedagogical purpose
setup_requirements: Sample AI policies from universities for analysis

review.value: Shneiderman's framework: AI systems should be reliable, safe, and trustworthy; humans should maintain meaningful control and oversight; accountability structures must be clear
Students need to understand: what AI can and cannot do, how it generates output, why it makes errors, how their data is used, and when AI use is appropriate vs. inappropriate

class_schedule.topic: Future Directions: AI and the Future of Teaching and Learning
learning_objectives: Evaluate predictions about AI's impact on education
Design for a future where AI capabilities continue to increase
Articulate what remains uniquely human in education
readings: Aoun, J. - Robot-Proof: Higher Education in the Age of AI Ch. 5-6
Brynjolfsson, E. & McAfee, A. - The Second Machine Age (education chapter)

lecture_notes.introduction: AI will continue to get more capable. Rather than asking 'will AI replace teachers?' we should ask: 'what aspects of education should AI do, what should humans do, and what should they do together?' This session explores the future through the lens of educational values.
key_concepts: What AI does well: personalization at scale, pattern recognition, content generation, tireless availability
What humans do uniquely: mentoring, motivating, modeling ethical reasoning, adapting to emotional context
Human-AI collaboration models for education
Preparing students for an AI-augmented workforce
Humanics: experiential learning, data literacy, human literacy, and technological literacy (Aoun)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Future scenario planning: create three scenarios (optimistic, pessimistic, realistic) for AI in education in 2035, identifying key decision points that determine which scenario unfolds
setup_requirements: Current trend data on AI capabilities and adoption

review.value: Aoun's framework for robot-proof education: combine data literacy (read the world of data), technological literacy (understand how machines work), and human literacy (communicate, think critically, exercise ethical judgment)
The most productive frame is not AI vs. teachers but AI + teachers: AI handles routine feedback, personalization, and content delivery; teachers focus on mentoring, motivation, and complex judgment

quizzes.topic: AI Foundations, Adaptive Learning, and NLP

questions.question: Explain Bayesian knowledge tracing using a concrete example. A student answers 3 math problems: correct, incorrect, correct. If the prior probability of mastery is 0.3, qualitatively describe how the model updates after each response.
questions.answer: BKT starts with P(mastered) = 0.3. After the correct response, the probability of mastery increases (maybe to ~0.5) because a correct answer is evidence of mastery, though the student could have guessed. After the incorrect response, the probability decreases (maybe to ~0.35) because an incorrect answer is evidence against mastery, though the student could have slipped. After the second correct response, the probability increases again (maybe to ~0.55). The magnitude of each update depends on the guess and slip parameters. If the guess rate is low, a correct answer is stronger evidence of mastery. If the slip rate is low, an incorrect answer is stronger evidence against mastery.

rubric.key: Correctly describes the updating direction after each response
Explains the role of guess and slip parameters
Demonstrates understanding of probabilistic updating, not deterministic

questions.question: What is 'hallucination' in the context of large language models, and why is it particularly dangerous in educational applications? Describe one specific educational scenario where hallucination could cause harm.
questions.answer: Hallucination is when an LLM generates text that is factually incorrect, fabricated, or nonsensical while presenting it with the same confident tone as accurate information. It is dangerous in education because students often cannot verify AI-generated content, and they may form incorrect mental models that are resistant to correction once established. Scenario: A medical student asks an AI tutor about drug interactions and receives a plausible but fabricated interaction warning. The student incorporates this false information into their clinical reasoning, potentially endangering patients. Unlike a textbook error that can be identified and corrected, each student may receive different hallucinated content, making systematic correction impossible.

rubric.key: Explains why it is particularly dangerous in education
Provides a specific, credible harm scenario

questions.question: An AI proctoring system flags 15% of African American students for 'suspicious behavior' during an online exam, compared to 3% of white students. Using the concepts of algorithmic bias and training data, explain the most likely cause and propose a solution.
questions.answer: The most likely cause is biased training data: facial recognition and anomaly detection models are trained predominantly on lighter-skinned faces, resulting in higher error rates for darker-skinned individuals. The system may also use environmental detection algorithms that flag students in certain home environments (lighting, background) that correlate with socioeconomic status and race. Solution: (1) Audit the system with a diverse test population and publish disaggregated accuracy data before deployment. (2) Raise the threshold for flagging to reduce false positives, accepting more false negatives. (3) Require human review of all flags before any academic consequences. (4) Consider alternative assessment integrity approaches that do not rely on facial recognition.

rubric.key: Identifies training data bias as the likely cause
Explains the mechanism (facial recognition performance gap)

questions.question: Explain the principle of 'human-in-the-loop' in educational AI. Give an example of an appropriate human-in-the-loop design and an example where removing the human from the loop would be harmful.
questions.answer: Human-in-the-loop means that AI systems provide recommendations, analysis, or drafts, but a human makes the final decision. Appropriate design: an AI system analyzes student submission patterns and flags students who may be at risk of failing. The instructor reviews the flags, considers context the AI does not have (family emergency, disability, already in touch), and decides which students to contact and how. Harmful removal: an automated system that directly sends academic probation notices to flagged students without instructor review. The AI may flag a student who submitted work late due to a documented accommodation, or a student who has already been in contact with the professor. The automated action could damage the student-instructor relationship and cause unnecessary distress.

rubric.key: Compelling example of harm from removing the human

major_assignments.title: AI-Enhanced Learning Experience Design
major_assignments.description: Design an AI-enhanced learning experience for a specific educational context. The design must include a needs analysis identifying where AI adds genuine value, a technical specification of the AI component, an ethical impact assessment, a human-centered interaction design, and an evaluation plan. You may prototype the experience using available AI tools (LLMs, adaptive platforms, or automation tools).
objectives: Apply human-centered AI design principles to an educational context
Conduct an ethical impact assessment for an AI educational intervention
Design AI interactions that maintain learner agency and transparency
Create an evaluation plan to measure both learning effectiveness and ethical compliance
deliverables: Design document (3,000 words) including needs analysis, technical spec, and interaction design
Ethical impact assessment using a structured framework
Prototype or detailed storyboard of the AI-enhanced experience
Draft AI-use policy for the context

criteria: Clearly identifies a problem where AI adds genuine value
Considers and rejects non-AI alternatives with justification
Target audience and context are well-defined
Human-centered design with appropriate human-in-the-loop mechanisms
Interaction design maintains transparency and learner agency
Identifies potential harms across all stakeholder groups
Addresses bias, privacy, and accountability concerns
Measures both learning effectiveness and ethical compliance
Addresses the unique challenges of evaluating AI-enhanced learning
Human-centered design with transparency and agency

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on AI concepts, applications, and ethics in education

participation.description: In-class AI tool explorations, ethical case discussions, and design critiques

midterm_examination.description: Covers AI foundations, adaptive learning, NLP, and ethical frameworks

final_project.description: Extended design project with prototype implementation and user testing

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected. Ethical case discussions and AI tool explorations are integral to learning. More than 3 absences will reduce the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 5% per day for up to 5 days. Design project milestones must be met for peer feedback cycles.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion and debate are central to this course. All written work must be individually authored. AI tools may be used as part of the learning process with disclosure.
course_policies.academic_integrity: You may use AI tools for research and prototyping but not for generating submitted writing without disclosure and critical evaluation. All sources must be cited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Computer with internet access. Access to AI tools (LLMs, adaptive learning platforms) provided through course accounts.
